summary: Khailo acknowledges that his peer-to-peer application Fire★ is technically inferior to Skype in nearly every measurable way, but argues it is 'infinitely better' because it is Free Software under GPL. The post celebrates the philosophical value of software freedom—transparency, user control, and privacy—over polished features backed by corporate resources.
key_points: Fire★ is a peer-to-peer application platform where users define their own modes of interaction using Lua
The author honestly admits his software is worse than Skype in features, usability, and stability
Free Software provides freedoms that proprietary software never will: transparency, modifiability, and privacy
The median number of contributors to a Free Software project is one, and the author accepts this reality
Success is redefined not by feature parity or market share but by the freedoms the software grants its users
money_quotes: You can think of it as an extensible Skype, where the modes of real time interaction are defined by the users and not the engineers.
It is infinitely better in a way Skype will never be because it is Free Software.
You can read it and know what it is doing. You can change it for the better. And you know it isn't spying on you.

I am a realist. It will never be better than Skype for communication and features. It doesn't have a multi billion dollar company behind it. However, I still consider it a success because it will benefit whoever uses it in a way that Skype can't.
key_insight: Software freedom is an axis of quality that no amount of corporate engineering can replicate, making even a rough Free Software tool infinitely superior to its polished proprietary counterpart in the ways that matter most.
